title: practical english usage - Part 334
content:
You can stay as long as you want.

I've been working here long enough. Time to get a new job.

The meeting went on much  too long.

few other common expressions.

However, long is used in affirmative clauses with too, enough, as and so, and in a 3 long     in  affirmative        clauses noi It takes a long time to get to her house, (    It lakes long...) I waited (for) a long time, but she didn't arrive. (\oi  I wailed-tong...) In affirmative clauses we  usually prefer (for) a long time.

2  (for)   a  long     time     in  affirmative        clauses She seldom stays long.

Have you been waiting long? It doesn't take long to get to her house.

clauses, and with restrictive words like hardly, seldom.

Long (meaning ‘ (for) a long time') is most common in questions and negative 1  long     in  questions       and   negatives 517  long     and   (for)   a  long     time ►     46i and (a) long     way        far ^367 and lonely          alone ►     s«o and little        small ►     isi and listen      (to)     hear He's unlikely to agree.

I'm likely to be busy tomorrow. Do you think it's likely to rain?

Be + (un)likely is often followed by an infinitive.

3  infinitive       after    be   (un)likely I thought it unlikely that she would come back.

It's likely that the meeting will go on late.

We   can use it as a preparatory subject or object for a that-dause (►    268-269).

2 it  is  (un)likely        + that-clause Word  Problems from A  to Z     Section 31 518 vocabulary •         look The team look to repeat their success. (= It looks as if they will. ..) Look + infinitive is also sometimes used in informal British English.

It looks like being a wet night. (= It looks as if it will be ...) Look like. . .ing... (informal) is used with future reference in British English.

She looks like she's dreaming, (informal) (     She-looks like dreaming.) She looks as if she's dreaming.

It looks as if it's going to rain, (     lt's-looking-as-if-.. .) She looks like her mother.

used in this case.

Look can be followed by like or as if (►  378). Progressive forms are not usually You look / You're looking very unhappy. What's the matter?

there is not much difference of meaning.

To  talk about a temporary appearance, we  can use simple or progressive forms; The garden looks a mess. (BrE) / looked a real fool when I fell in the river. (BrE) not You look angry - what's the matter? (     Y&u-leote-angrily-...) be followed by adjectives or (in British English) nouns.

Look can mean 'seem' or ‘ appear’ . In this case it is a linking verb (►   1 1) and can 1 linking     verb    (= 'seem') 518  look Much,  ninny and far are also more common in questions and negative clauses (►   165. 461).

For no longer, ►     535.

I hope you'll stay longer next time, (     . . .fier^longpr-lime/) l'he comparative of for a long time is (for) longer.

‘ How long have you been here for?' ‘ Since last Monday.' ‘ How long are you here for?' ‘ Until the end of next week.' Questions like How long are you here for? refer to the future. Compare: 5 How    long     are    you     here     for?

of the clause).

for a long time is outside the influence of not (it could go at the beginning and third sentences, not goes with for long, but in the second and fourth The  reason for the difference is to do with the ‘ scope of negation’ : in the first for a long time’  - the same as For a long time he didn't work).

He didn't work for a long time. (This could mean ‘ He was unemployed - He didn't work for long. (= He soon stopped working.) time' - the same as For a long time she didn't speak).

She didn't speak for a long time. (This could mean ‘ She was silent for a long - She didn't speak for long. (= She only spoke for a short time.) meaning from for long. Compare: When  for ɑ  long time is used in a negative clause, it sometimes has a different 4 for    a  long     time     in  negative       clauses vocabulary •  520  loudly and aloud ‘ What did you say? ‘ Oh,  nothing. I was just thinking aloud.' She has a very good pronunciation when she reads aloud.
